nativeRange.habitat: Found in shaded lagoons and reef slopes, where it attaches to hard substrate on cave ceilings, overhangs, and ledges that receive minimal direct light.

decorPreferences: live rock with overhangs and caves
shaded placement away from direct light
can be mounted upside-down on cave ceiling

diet.notes: The Chili Coral is fully non-photosynthetic — it contains no symbiotic algae and relies entirely on active filter feeding for nutrition. It requires daily target feeding with small zooplankton such as rotifers, baby brine shrimp nauplii, copepods, cyclops, oyster eggs, prawn eggs, micro-plankton, and prepared non-photosynthetic coral foods such as Cyclopeeze and Dustfood. Polyps tend to expand and feed most actively after lights-out. Consistent, heavy feeding is the single most critical factor in long-term success with this species.

goodWith: photosynthetic soft corals with adequate spacing
avoidWith: aggressive LPS corals with sweeper tentacles placed in direct contact
SPS corals in ultra-low nutrient (zeovit) systems
bright direct light from intense reef fixtures

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by Josuevg. Vivid red Nephthyigorgia in a reef aquarium; correctly ID'd chili coral showing the diagnostic scarlet branching non-photosynthetic body with polyp-covered fingers. Sharp and in-colour but low resolution (265x233) — only commercial-friendly live in-water photo of the genus found.

summary: The Chili Coral (Nephthyigorgia sp.) is a vivid red non-photosynthetic soft coral that gets its color from pigment rather than symbiotic algae. Among non-photosynthetic corals it is considered one of the more accessible species, though expert-level feeding commitment is still required.
careNotes:
The Chili Coral is a non-photosynthetic (azooxanthellate) soft coral belonging to the family Nephthyigorgiidae, which was formerly classified within Nidaliidae before post-2020 molecular work established it as a distinct family. It is generally considered one of the more attainable non-photosynthetic soft corals, though it still demands expert-level commitment to feeding.

Key care requirements:

• Lighting: Does not require light for survival and actively prefers shade. Best placed under a cave ceiling, overhang, or ledge where direct light does not reach. Direct illumination causes the coral to retract, facilitates unwanted algae growth on the body, and causes long-term decline. PAR values of 20–80 or lower are appropriate; this species is unsuitable for the bright upper zones designed for SPS.

• Flow: Moderate-to-strong indirect flow is essential — good water movement prevents detritus settling on the colony, delivers suspended food particles to the polyps, and keeps the tissue clean. Chaotic, alternating flow (wavemaker or alternating powerheads) is preferable to constant laminar flow. Despite the flow requirement, it has been documented thriving in somewhat lower flow than other NPS species.

• Feeding: This is the primary challenge. The Chili Coral must be fed every day (or at minimum every other day) with appropriately sized zooplankton: rotifers, baby brine shrimp nauplii, copepods, Cyclopeeze, oyster eggs, fish eggs, prawn eggs, Daphnia, Dustfood, or equivalent prepared NPS foods. Target feeding directly to the colony is strongly recommended. Polyps typically remain retracted during the day — a well-fed specimen will show extended polyps most consistently after lights-out. Plan for elevated nitrate/phosphate and more frequent water changes to manage the organic load from daily feeding.

• Tank maturity: An established aquarium (generally 6+ months) with stable water chemistry and an existing pod/plankton population is advisable. Nutrient-poor zeovit-style systems are poorly suited to this species.

• Copper sensitivity: Nephthyigorgia is considered sensitive to copper-based medications. Copper treatments should not be used in any tank housing this species.

• Placement flexibility: Unlike many corals, the Chili Coral can be attached in any orientation — horizontally, vertically, or upside-down — as long as the position provides shade and good indirect flow. It is often described as suitable for the 'dead zones' of the aquascape that receive little light.

• Taxonomy note: Some vendors still list this coral under Scleronephthya or Alcyonium in older literature; the genus name Nephthyigorgia sp. is the most reliable search term when sourcing it.
breedingNotes: Captive propagation of Nephthyigorgia sp. is not commonly practiced or documented in the hobby. Like other azooxanthellate soft corals, slow natural fragmentation or basal spreading can occur in ideal conditions, but deliberate fragging is rarely reported. No established captive breeding protocol is available.

primary.name: LiveAquaria – Chili Coral
primary.notes: Primary source for water parameters (temp 72–78°F, SG 1.023–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12), care level (Expert Only), lighting (low), flow (medium to strong), placement (bottom/shaded), feeding foods and temperament (peaceful).

additional.name: AlgaeBarn – A Look at the Chili Coral
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult colony size (≤6 in / ~15 cm), native range (Indo-Pacific lagoons and shaded reef slopes), flow (moderate-to-strong chaotic), feeding foods and method (target feeding, brine shrimp cysts, phytoplankton). Corroborates daily feeding requirement.

additional.name: Reeflex – Nephthyigorgia sp.
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (23–26°C), adult size (up to 12 cm), taxonomy (family Nephthyigorgiidae), care level (experts only), native range (Indonesia), feeding foods list. Minor disagreement: Reeflex upper temp 26°C vs LiveAquaria 78°F (25.6°C) — effectively identical; used 23–26°C.

additional.name: TankFacts – Red Chili Coral
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters: temp 76–80°F (24.4–26.7°C), SG 1.023–1.026, pH 7.8–8.4, kH 8–12 dKH, Ca 380–450 ppm, Mg 1200–1400 ppm. Slightly higher salinity upper (1.026) and pH lower (7.8) than LiveAquaria; averages adopted for pH (8.1 lower bound) and salinity (1.025 upper).

additional.name: Pod Your Reef – Mastering NPS Corals
additional.notes: Confirmed daily feeding requirement, strong flow preference, shade placement, and that Chili Coral is among the more accessible NPS species. Foods: brine nauplii, Cyclopeeze, rotifers. After-lights-out feeding behaviour confirmed.
